tasks.title: Warm-up: Node Labeling and Placement Control with nodeSelector
tasks.goal: Use kubectl label to attach a label to a node, then use nodeSelector to ensure a Pod is placed only on nodes carrying that label. Verify the placement with -o wide, and experience firsthand what causes Pending when no matching label exists.
tasks.prompt:
1. List all nodes in the cluster and inspect their current labels.
2. Choose one node and attach the label disktype=ssd to it.
3. Write a Pod manifest (nodeselector-pod.yaml) with a nodeSelector targeting disktype=ssd nodes, then deploy it.
   - Image: nginx:1.25
4. Run kubectl get pod -o wide to confirm the Pod is running on the labeled node.
5. Deliberately change the label to one that does not exist (disktype=nvme), create a second Pod, and confirm it enters Pending state. Use kubectl describe pod to find the event message.
6. After the exercise, remove the disktype label from the first node.
tasks.starter:
# 1. List nodes and their labels
kubectl get nodes --show-labels

# 2. Attach the disktype=ssd label (replace <node-name> with the actual node name from above)
kubectl label nodes <node-name> disktype=ssd

# 3. nodeselector-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ssd-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    ____: ____
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25

# 5. Pod for Pending test (nodeselector-pending.yaml)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nvme-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ____   # fill in a value that does not exist
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25
tasks.solution:
# 1. List nodes
kubectl get nodes --show-labels

# 2. Attach the label (replace with your actual node name, e.g. minikube or node1)
kubectl label nodes minikube disktype=ssd

# Verify the label was applied
kubectl get node minikube --show-labels | grep disktype

# 3. nodeselector-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ssd-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ssd
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25

# Apply
kubectl apply -f nodeselector-pod.yaml

# 4. Verify placement
kubectl get pod ssd-pod -o wide
# The NODE column must show the node you labeled with disktype=ssd

# 5. Pending test
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nvme-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: nvme
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25
EOF

kubectl get pod nvme-pod   # STATUS: Pending
kubectl describe pod nvme-pod
# Look for a message similar to this in the Events section:
# "0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) didn't match Pod's node affinity/selector."

# 6. Clean up
kubectl delete pod ssd-pod nvme-pod
kubectl label nodes minikube disktype-   # remove label: append a hyphen after the key
tasks.explanation: nodeSelector is the simplest node-selection mechanism available in a PodSpec. Only nodes that carry every specified key=value label are considered candidates; if no node satisfies the constraint, the Pod cannot leave Pending state. The 'didn't match Pod's node affinity/selector' message in the Events section of kubectl describe pod tells you the scheduler found zero feasible nodes during the filtering phase. The rule for removing a label is to append a hyphen (-) after the key: kubectl label nodes <node> disktype-.
hints: Find the node name in the NAME column of kubectl get nodes, then use that exact name with kubectl label nodes <node-name>. In a single-node minikube environment, the node is typically named 'minikube'.
The indentation under nodeSelector is 4 spaces relative to spec. Each entry follows the format 'key: value'; multiple entries are all ANDed together.
If the NODE column in kubectl get pod -o wide shows <none> or STATUS is Pending, read the Events section of kubectl describe pod <name> to find the message left by the scheduler.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Node Affinity — Comparing Hard and Soft Constraints
tasks.goal: Write Node Affinity rules using both requiredDuringScheduling (hard) and preferredDuringScheduling (soft) and observe the difference in behavior directly. Experience that a hard constraint causes Pending when no matching node exists, while a soft preference still results in a Running Pod placed on another node.
tasks.prompt:
1. Attach the label environment=production to a node.
2. Write a hard Node Affinity Pod manifest (node-affinity-hard.yaml).
   - operator: In, values: ["production"]
   - Image: nginx:1.25
   - Deploy and verify which node the Pod was placed on.
3. Remove the environment label from the node.
4. Write a soft Node Affinity Pod manifest (node-affinity-soft.yaml).
   - preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution, weight: 80
   - Same environment=production preference condition
   - Deploy and confirm the Pod reaches Running even without the label present.
5. Compare the placement results of both Pods using kubectl get pod -o wide.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Attach the label
kubectl label nodes <node-name> environment=production

# 2. node-affinity-hard.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: affinity-hard-pod
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
          - matchExpressions:
              - key: environment
                operator: ____
                values:
                  - ____
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25

# 4. node-affinity-soft.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: affinity-soft-pod
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      ____:
        - weight: ____
          preference:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: environment
                operator: In
                values:
                  - production
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25
tasks.solution:
# 1. Attach the label
kubectl label nodes minikube environment=production

# 2. node-affinity-hard.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: affinity-hard-pod
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
          - matchExpressions:
              - key: environment
                operator: In
                values:
                  - production
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f node-affinity-hard.yaml
kubectl get pod affinity-hard-pod -o wide   # Running, confirm placement on labeled node

# 3. Remove the label
kubectl label nodes minikube environment-

# 4. node-affinity-soft.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: affinity-soft-pod
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        - weight: 80
          preference:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: environment
                operator: In
                values:
                  - production
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f node-affinity-soft.yaml

# 5. Compare
kubectl get pod affinity-hard-pod affinity-soft-pod -o wide
# affinity-hard-pod: was placed before the label was removed, so it stays Running
#   (IgnoredDuringExecution — the rule is not re-evaluated for already-running Pods)
# affinity-soft-pod: Running on a node without the label
#   (preferred is a hint, not a requirement — Pod is placed elsewhere if no match)

# Clean up
kubectl delete pod affinity-hard-pod affinity-soft-pod
tasks.explanation: requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution participates in the scheduler's filtering phase (hard constraint). If no node satisfies the condition, the Pod stays Pending. preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution participates in the scoring phase (soft preference) and uses weight to express preference strength. When the preferred node is unavailable, the Pod is placed on another node and reaches Running. The IgnoredDuringExecution suffix means 'do not re-apply this rule to Pods that are already running.' Removing the label after scheduling therefore does not evict an already-running Pod. In production, use preferred as the default for better availability, and reserve required for workloads that must be isolated to a specific node group (e.g., GPU-only, compliance requirements).
hints: Under requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution, the structure is nodeSelectorTerms (array) → matchExpressions (array). Under preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution, the structure jumps directly to weight and preference, so the indentation levels differ between the two forms.

Valid operator values: In (matches any value in the list), NotIn (matches none of the values), Exists (key must be present), DoesNotExist (key must be absent). For a simple 'label equals this value' check, use In with a single-element values list.

It is expected behavior for the hard-constraint Pod to remain Running after the label is removed. That is exactly what IgnoredDuringExecution means. Creating a new Pod after removing the label will produce Pending, which demonstrates the rule.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Spreading Replicas with Pod Anti-Affinity
tasks.goal: Apply podAntiAffinity to a replicas: 2 Deployment to force the two Pods onto different nodes. In a single-node environment, confirm that using required causes the second Pod to go Pending, then switch to preferred to resolve it.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write a Deployment manifest (anti-affinity-deploy.yaml) with replicas: 2 and a required podAntiAffinity condition.
   - Selector/labels: app=web-ha
   - topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
   - Image: nginx:1.25
2. After applying, use kubectl get pod -o wide to check the placement status of both Pods.
   - In a single-node environment, observe that the second Pod goes Pending.
3. Run kubectl describe pod <Pending-Pod> to find the Anti-Affinity violation message.
4. Change required to preferred (weight: 100), update the Deployment, and confirm the previously Pending Pod becomes Running.
tasks.starter:
# anti-affinity-deploy.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-ha
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-ha
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-ha
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            - labelSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  app: ____    # reject co-location with Pods that share this label
              topologyKey: ____
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: nginx:1.25
tasks.solution:
# anti-affinity-deploy.yaml — required version
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-ha
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-ha
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-ha
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            - labelSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  app: web-ha
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f anti-affinity-deploy.yaml
kubectl get pod -o wide
# Single-node environment: first Pod Running, second Pod Pending

# Find the Pending Pod name, then describe it
kubectl describe pod <Pending-Pod-name>
# Expected event message:
# "0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) didn't match pod anti-affinity rules."

# Switch required -> preferred (edit the file and re-apply)
# Replace this block in anti-affinity-deploy.yaml:
#   requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
#     - labelSelector: ...
# with:
#   preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
#     - weight: 100
#       podAffinityTerm:
#         labelSelector:
#           matchLabels:
#             app: web-ha
#         topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname

# Complete preferred version:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-ha
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-ha
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-ha
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            - weight: 100
              podAffinityTerm:
                labelSelector:
                  matchLabels:
                    app: web-ha
                topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f anti-affinity-deploy.yaml
kubectl get pod -o wide
# Both Pods Running (co-located on the same node if only one node is available)

# Clean up
kubectl delete deployment web-ha
tasks.explanation: The labelSelector in podAntiAffinity means 'avoid any node that already has a Pod with this label.' topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname interprets 'same' as 'same node (hostname).' Switching topologyKey to topology.kubernetes.io/zone spreads Pods across availability zones instead. With required, if the cluster has fewer nodes than replicas, the extra Pods get stuck in Pending. With preferred, the rule becomes 'place on a different node if possible, but co-locate if no other option exists,' which resolves the Pending state. When node failure resilience is the goal, use required on a multi-node cluster. For local development environments with a single node, use preferred.
hints: The required structure: podAntiAffinity.requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution contains array items with labelSelector and topologyKey directly. The preferred structure wraps these inside weight and podAffinityTerm, so the indentation levels are different — be careful.
kubernetes.io/hostname is a built-in label automatically applied to every node. Run kubectl get node minikube --show-labels | grep hostname to see the actual value.
Find the Pending Pod quickly with kubectl get pod | grep Pending. In the Events output, look for a line containing 'anti-affinity' to pinpoint the cause immediately.

tasks.title: Advanced: Dedicated Node Setup with Taint/Toleration + Node Affinity
tasks.goal: Simulate a GPU-dedicated node: use a Taint to block ordinary Pods, then demonstrate that only a Pod carrying both a Toleration and Node Affinity is reliably pinned to that node. Confirm that a Toleration alone can still land on other nodes, making the role distinction between the two mechanisms clear.
tasks.prompt:
1. Apply both a dedicated=gpu label and a dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule Taint to a node simultaneously.
2. Deploy a plain Pod (no-toleration-pod.yaml) onto the tainted node and confirm it is rejected.
3. Deploy a Pod with only a Toleration (toleration-only-pod.yaml).
   - Toleration: key=dedicated, operator=Equal, value=gpu, effect=NoSchedule
   - On a multi-node cluster, observe that the scheduler may still place it on a non-GPU node.
4. Write a Pod (gpu-pod.yaml) that combines a Toleration with Node Affinity (required, targeting the dedicated=gpu label).
   - key: dedicated, operator: In, values: ["gpu"]
   - After deploying, confirm it runs exclusively on the node with the GPU label.
5. Remove the Taint and clean up.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Prepare the node (replace <node-name> with your actual node name)
kubectl label nodes <node-name> dedicated=gpu
kubectl taint nodes <node-name> dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule

# Verify the Taint
kubectl describe node <node-name> | grep -i taint

# 2. no-toleration-pod.yaml — plain Pod (blocked by the Taint)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-toleration-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.25

# 3. toleration-only-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: toleration-only-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
    - key: "____"
      operator: "____"
      value: "____"
      effect: "____"
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.25

# 4. gpu-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: gpu-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
    - key: "dedicated"
      operator: "Equal"
      value: "gpu"
      effect: "NoSchedule"
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
          - matchExpressions:
              - key: ____
                operator: In
                values:
                  - ____
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.25
tasks.solution:
# 1. Prepare the node
kubectl label nodes minikube dedicated=gpu
kubectl taint nodes minikube dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule

# Verify the Taint
kubectl describe node minikube | grep -i taint
# Expected output: Taints: dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule

# 2. Deploy a plain Pod — blocked by the Taint
kubectl apply -f no-toleration-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod no-toleration-pod
# STATUS: Pending
kubectl describe pod no-toleration-pod
# Events: "0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) had untolerated taint {dedicated: gpu}."

# 3. Pod with Toleration only
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: toleration-only-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
    - key: "dedicated"
      operator: "Equal"
      value: "gpu"
      effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f toleration-only-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod toleration-only-pod -o wide
# Single-node environment: lands on the GPU node (minikube) — only candidate available
# Multi-node environment: may be placed on a non-GPU node — a Toleration is permission, not a directive

# 4. Pod with Toleration + Node Affinity — pinned exclusively to the GPU node
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: gpu-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
    - key: "dedicated"
      operator: "Equal"
      value: "gpu"
      effect: "NoSchedule"
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
          - matchExpressions:
              - key: dedicated
                operator: In
                values:
                  - gpu
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: nginx:1.25

kubectl apply -f gpu-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod gpu-pod -o wide
# The NODE column must show the node labeled dedicated=gpu

# 5. Clean up
kubectl delete pod no-toleration-pod toleration-only-pod gpu-pod
kubectl taint nodes minikube dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule-   # remove Taint: append hyphen
kubectl label nodes minikube dedicated-                   # remove label
tasks.explanation: The Taint (lock) and Toleration (key) relationship: a NoSchedule Taint on a node completely blocks any Pod that lacks a matching Toleration during the filtering phase. To remove a Taint, run kubectl taint nodes <name> <key>=<value>:<effect>- with a trailing hyphen. An important misconception to avoid: a Toleration grants permission — 'this Pod may run on this node' — but does not force it there. A Pod with only a Toleration is free to land on any ordinary node that has no Taint. To create a truly dedicated node for expensive resources like GPUs, you need both Taint (to block outside Pods) and Node Affinity (to actively pull your Pods onto that node). Using the NoExecute effect adds another dimension: it also evicts existing Pods on the node that lack a Toleration, so check the impact carefully before applying it to a running node.
hints: Applying a Taint: kubectl taint nodes <node> <key>=<value>:<effect> (e.g., dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule). Removing it: append a hyphen at the end — dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule-. Omitting the colon (:) before the effect will produce an error.

If toleration-only-pod lands on the GPU node in a single-node environment, that is expected — it is the only available candidate. On a multi-node cluster, the scheduler may choose a non-GPU node instead, which illustrates that a Toleration is not a forced placement directive.

In gpu-pod, the key, value, and effect in the Toleration must exactly match the Taint on the node, including case. Run kubectl describe node <name> | grep Taint to double-check the Taint values before writing the Toleration.
